title: Flip the execute bit
prompt: Files carry permissions: read, write, execute. A script won't run without the execute permission. run.sh is in your lab but not executable. Check it with ls -l (look at the rwx letters), make it executable with chmod +x, then run it with ./run.sh — it prints the flag.

checks.command: test -x run.sh && echo executable || echo locked

details.restate: Two steps: give run.sh the execute permission, then actually run it. It prints the flag when it runs. The ./ prefix means 'the one in this folder'.

io.because: no x among the letters means no execute permission yet

terms.meaning: run the file from the current folder — typing just run.sh searches the system PATH instead and won't find it

help.plain: The script is fine — it just isn't allowed to run yet. Grant the permission, then execute it from this folder.
help.analogy: A key that hasn't been cut yet: the metal is all there, but until the locksmith cuts it (chmod +x), it turns nothing.
help.mnemonic: chmod +x makes it go; ./ says 'the one right here'.
help.nudge: ls -l shows rw- but no x. Which command adds permissions, and how do you run a script that lives in the current folder?
